prompt: What are the investment philosophies of Duan Yongping, Warren Buffett, and Charlie Munger?
article:
## Short answer

**Duan Yongping, Warren Buffett, and Charlie Munger start from the same first principle:** a stock is **not** a trading token but a **fractional ownership interest in a business**, and the relevant question is what that business can **distribute, reinvest, and compound in cash terms over time**. Their differences are not about whether value matters, but about **where they place the analytical weight**. **Buffett** turns the idea into a full capital-allocation system built on intrinsic value, business quality, management, and balance-sheet resilience. **Munger** expands it into a broader theory of judgment using multidisciplinary mental models, inversion, opportunity cost, and psychology. **Duan** applies the Buffett-Munger framework through an operator’s lens that gives unusually high weight to **business model clarity, corporate culture, user value, and ruthless self-restraint about what he does not understand**. [[1]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf) [[7]](https://www.lifestories.org/attachment/en/5b64a8a46aa72ce4430396a1/TextOneColumnWithFile/62b4d09ad8e78ac4930df508)(https://www.lifestories.org/attachment/en/5b64a8a46aa72ce4430396a1/TextOneColumnWithFile/62b4d09ad8e78ac4930df508) [[8]](https://xueqiu.com/u/1247347556)(https://xueqiu.com/u/1247347556) [[11]](https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579)(https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579) [[46]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html) [[67]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/owners.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/owners.html) [[71]](https://speakola.com/corp/charli-munger-widom-business-usc-1994)(https://speakola.com/corp/charli-munger-widom-business-usc-1994)

The deepest commonality is therefore conceptual, not stylistic: all three think the investor’s main job is **judgment under uncertainty**, not activity. Their shared edge comes less from finding many ideas than from avoiding predictable errors: buying what is outside one’s competence, paying up for fragile economics, using leverage that can force liquidation, or letting crowd enthusiasm substitute for analysis. [[1]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf) [[2]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf) [[6]](https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf)(https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf) [[9]](https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891)(https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891) [[11]](https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579)(https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579) [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do) [[45]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1985.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1985.html) [[75]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)

## How to read their “investment philosophy”

For this question, **investment philosophy** means the principles that govern **what they buy, what they avoid, how they define risk, how long they hold, what role management and culture play, and how they react to uncertainty and price volatility**. It is **not** just a list of current holdings. Berkshire’s letters, Munger’s speeches/interviews, and Duan’s own Xueqiu statements or direct interview remarks are therefore more authoritative than portfolio snapshots alone. [[1]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf) [[6]](https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf)(https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf) [[7]](https://www.lifestories.org/attachment/en/5b64a8a46aa72ce4430396a1/TextOneColumnWithFile/62b4d09ad8e78ac4930df508)(https://www.lifestories.org/attachment/en/5b64a8a46aa72ce4430396a1/TextOneColumnWithFile/62b4d09ad8e78ac4930df508) [[8]](https://xueqiu.com/u/1247347556)(https://xueqiu.com/u/1247347556) [[15]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/)

That source distinction matters especially for **Duan**. Berkshire and Munger left a long archive of primary materials; Duan’s public record is more fragmented, and many widely circulated formulations of his views are **secondary compilations** of Xueqiu posts or summaries of interviews rather than official long-form letters. The safest interpretation is therefore to treat his own account statements and direct interview transcripts as highest authority, and portfolio snapshots or reposted summaries as supporting evidence rather than as philosophy in themselves. [[8]](https://xueqiu.com/u/1247347556)(https://xueqiu.com/u/1247347556) [[11]](https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579)(https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579)

A useful first synthesis is that all three treat investing as a process of **selection and omission**: the edge comes less from constant activity than from **knowing what not to do**—not buying what you do not understand, not overpaying for fragile economics, not using dangerous leverage, and not letting market emotion drive decisions. [[1]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf) [[2]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf) [[6]](https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf)(https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf) [[9]](https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891)(https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891) [[11]](https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579)(https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579) [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do) [[45]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1985.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1985.html) [[75]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)

---

## Duan Yongping’s investment philosophy

## 1) “Buying stocks is buying companies”

Duan’s most repeated principle is that **“buying stocks is buying companies”**, and in fuller form, **buying a company means buying its discounted future cash flows**. He has explicitly said his understanding of investing is consistent with Buffett’s because investing is, in essence, **value investing**. [[8]](https://xueqiu.com/u/1247347556)(https://xueqiu.com/u/1247347556) [[11]](https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579)(https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579) [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do) [[37]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/51820977/duan-yongping-rarely-makes-an-appearance-discussing-anecdotes-about-buffett)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/51820977/duan-yongping-rarely-makes-an-appearance-discussing-anecdotes-about-buffett)

He is unusually blunt about how rarely investors truly operate this way. In recent interview summaries, he says **less than 1%** of people really understand what “buying stocks is buying companies” means, and that actually acting on that statement is even harder. [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[28]](https://www.binance.com/en/square/post/32324883372258)(https://www.binance.com/en/square/post/32324883372258) That claim is not rhetorical exaggeration; it is a diagnosis of the gap between **verbal agreement** and **decision behavior**. Many investors say they are “long term,” but still anchor on quarterly price moves, relative performance, or narrative momentum rather than on business economics.

This matters because it shifts the operative question from “Will the stock go up?” to **“What kind of business is this, what cash can it produce, and how certain am I about that?”**. Duan therefore treats price as meaningful only **after** the business is understood. [[11]](https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579)(https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579) [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do) [[39]](https://www.futunn.com/en/learn/detail-duan-yongping-dont-do-what-you-dont-understand-when-investing-1259-2109010075)(https://www.futunn.com/en/learn/detail-duan-yongping-dont-do-what-you-dont-understand-when-investing-1259-2109010075)

In his 2018 Stanford talk translation, he reduced the whole buying process to **two filters**: **understandable** and **cheap relative to value**. [[38]](https://finance.yahoo.com/news/notes-duan-yongpings-talk-stanford-160952171.html)(https://finance.yahoo.com/news/notes-duan-yongpings-talk-stanford-160952171.html) That compression is important. It means that Duan’s philosophy is not a sprawling checklist but a very narrow funnel: first eliminate almost everything that cannot be understood with confidence; then insist on price discipline on the small remainder.

## 2) Circle of competence: narrow by design, not by embarrassment

Duan is unusually explicit that the most important thing is not having a large circle of competence but **knowing its boundaries**. He says the investor must be honest with himself about what he knows and does not know, and that the real meaning of competence is the range within which one can judge future cash flows with some confidence. [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do) [[38]](https://finance.yahoo.com/news/notes-duan-yongpings-talk-stanford-160952171.html)(https://finance.yahoo.com/news/notes-duan-yongpings-talk-stanford-160952171.html)

He turns this into a **“stop doing list”**:  
- **Do not invest in what you do not understand**. [[9]](https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891)(https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891) [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)  
- **Do not use leverage / margin**. [[9]](https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891)(https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891) [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do) [[38]](https://finance.yahoo.com/news/notes-duan-yongpings-talk-stanford-160952171.html)(https://finance.yahoo.com/news/notes-duan-yongpings-talk-stanford-160952171.html)  
- **Do not short**. [[9]](https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891)(https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891) [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)  
- **Do not chase market hot spots**. [[9]](https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891)(https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891)

That is not rhetorical minimalism; it is his risk system. Duan argues that many people lose money not because they lack IQ but because they **wander outside their competence** and then add leverage or imitation on top of ignorance. [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do) The causal chain is straightforward: once the underlying business is poorly understood, the investor cannot distinguish a falling price caused by temporary sentiment from a falling price caused by real business deterioration. At that point, leverage and imitation turn uncertainty into forced action.

He also offers a practical competence test: if a stock falls and you immediately want to sell, you probably **did not understand it well enough**; if you can imagine owning the business privately for ten years and sleeping well, you probably understand it better. [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do) That is psychologically astute. A sell impulse after volatility often reveals not risk intolerance alone, but **epistemic weakness**: the investor never had a business-level thesis strong enough to absorb price movement.

## 3) Margin of safety is not just “cheapness”

One of Duan’s most distinctive contributions is his interpretation of **margin of safety**. In recent interview summaries, he says Buffett’s margin of safety **does not simply mean a low price**; it means **how well you understand the company**. In that framing, **understanding itself is a safety cushion**. [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[28]](https://www.binance.com/en/square/post/32324883372258)(https://www.binance.com/en/square/post/32324883372258) [[42]](https://www.panewslab.com/en/articles/e3681069-bdd4-4d57-8370-0ea7ebbb110a)(https://www.panewslab.com/en/articles/e3681069-bdd4-4d57-8370-0ea7ebbb110a)

He does **not** reject valuation. He has also said that intrinsic value is the **discounted value of future cash flows**, that stocks should be bought when price is below intrinsic value, and that the required discount depends partly on **opportunity cost**. He adds that discounted cash flow is not a calculator trick but a **way of thinking**. [[11]](https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579)(https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579) [[39]](https://www.futunn.com/en/learn/detail-duan-yongping-dont-do-what-you-dont-understand-when-investing-1259-2109010075)(https://www.futunn.com/en/learn/detail-duan-yongping-dont-do-what-you-dont-understand-when-investing-1259-2109010075)

The synthesis is important: for Duan, safety has **two layers**:  
1. **Cognitive margin of safety** — do I truly understand the business? [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[28]](https://www.binance.com/en/square/post/32324883372258)(https://www.binance.com/en/square/post/32324883372258) [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)  
2. **Price margin of safety** — am I buying below what the business is worth? [[11]](https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579)(https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579) [[39]](https://www.futunn.com/en/learn/detail-duan-yongping-dont-do-what-you-dont-understand-when-investing-1259-2109010075)(https://www.futunn.com/en/learn/detail-duan-yongping-dont-do-what-you-dont-understand-when-investing-1259-2109010075)

That is why he warns that **cheap things can get cheaper**. A low multiple without real understanding is not safety; it may just be a trap. [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[42]](https://www.panewslab.com/en/articles/e3681069-bdd4-4d57-8370-0ea7ebbb110a)(https://www.panewslab.com/en/articles/e3681069-bdd4-4d57-8370-0ea7ebbb110a) In practice, this pushes Duan away from the classic error of buying “statistically cheap” businesses with weak competitive positions, unclear governance, or deteriorating user economics.

## 4) Business quality = business model + culture

Duan often says that understanding focuses mainly on **two things: business model and corporate culture**. [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)

That is where his background as a founder/operator shows up most clearly. He does not analyze businesses only as financial statements; he asks:  
- **How does this company actually make money?** [[11]](https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579)(https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579) [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)  
- **Why do customers stick with it?** [[9]](https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891)(https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891) [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[30]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tengjiajimmyli_duan-yongping-a-chinese-american-billionaire-activity-7394915600617099264-s6EF)(https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tengjiajimmyli_duan-yongping-a-chinese-american-billionaire-activity-7394915600617099264-s6EF)  
- **What makes the product meaningfully different?** [[50]](https://www.zhihu.com/en/answer/3336576532)(https://www.zhihu.com/en/answer/3336576532)  
- **Does the culture correct mistakes and keep management aligned with users?** [[9]](https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891)(https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891) [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[28]](https://www.binance.com/en/square/post/32324883372258)(https://www.binance.com/en/square/post/32324883372258) [[38]](https://finance.yahoo.com/news/notes-duan-yongpings-talk-stanford-160952171.html)(https://finance.yahoo.com/news/notes-duan-yongpings-talk-stanford-160952171.html)

He has argued that good culture does not mean a company never makes mistakes; it means the company can **return to the right path** after mistakes because it has a kind of internal “North Star.” [[9]](https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891)(https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891) [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[28]](https://www.binance.com/en/square/post/32324883372258)(https://www.binance.com/en/square/post/32324883372258) That is a stronger claim than the usual “culture matters.” Duan is saying culture is valuable because it changes operating behavior in concrete ways: product choices stay user-centered, managers correct rather than conceal mistakes, and the firm is less likely to destroy a moat through short-term extraction.

This is why Duan can sound “more qualitative” than spreadsheet value investors. For him, culture is not soft fluff; it is a mechanism for whether a moat can **persist and self-repair**. [[9]](https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891)(https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891) [[11]](https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579)(https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579) [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)

This is also where his China-context adaptation becomes clearer. In markets where industry structure can shift quickly and governance quality varies widely across firms, Duan leans even harder on **simple business models, founder/operator quality, product truth, and culture continuity**. He is not “changing value investing” so much as tightening the filters that matter most when externally visible metrics alone are insufficient.

## 5) Long-term ownership, but not blind “never sell”

Duan is a long-term investor, but not a slogan investor. He has said that after buying a company he does not intend to sell **unless** the company’s profitability deteriorates or there is a **better use of capital**. He also says many people misunderstand buy-and-hold: long-term investing does **not** mean “never sell”; it means hold as long as the business and opportunity cost justify it. [[28]](https://www.binance.com/en/square/post/32324883372258)(https://www.binance.com/en/square/post/32324883372258) [[30]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tengjiajimmyli_duan-yongping-a-chinese-american-billionaire-activity-7394915600617099264-s6EF)(https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tengjiajimmyli_duan-yongping-a-chinese-american-billionaire-activity-7394915600617099264-s6EF) [[38]](https://finance.yahoo.com/news/notes-duan-yongpings-talk-stanford-160952171.html)(https://finance.yahoo.com/news/notes-duan-yongpings-talk-stanford-160952171.html)

That makes his philosophy strongly **opportunity-cost-based**, which is very Munger-like. The question is never merely “Is this still good?” but also **“Is this still the best place for my capital?”**. [[11]](https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579)(https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579) [[30]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tengjiajimmyli_duan-yongping-a-chinese-american-billionaire-activity-7394915600617099264-s6EF)(https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tengjiajimmyli_duan-yongping-a-chinese-american-billionaire-activity-7394915600617099264-s6EF)

In practice, this also explains why he can use tools such as options without changing his philosophy: when used against businesses he already wants to own, the option is not the thesis; it is a way to improve entry economics or get paid to wait. The underlying discipline still comes from business understanding and valuation, not from trading the derivative itself.

## 6) Temperament: rationality, sleep, and immediate error correction

Duan repeatedly says investing is **simple, but not easy**, because remaining rational is hard. [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[42]](https://www.panewslab.com/en/articles/e3681069-bdd4-4d57-8370-0ea7ebbb110a)(https://www.panewslab.com/en/articles/e3681069-bdd4-4d57-8370-0ea7ebbb110a)

His behavioral rules are practical:  
- Buy only what lets you **sleep peacefully**. [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)  
- If you cannot tolerate a **50% drawdown**, you should not own the stock. [[42]](https://www.panewslab.com/en/articles/e3681069-bdd4-4d57-8370-0ea7ebbb110a)(https://www.panewslab.com/en/articles/e3681069-bdd4-4d57-8370-0ea7ebbb110a) [[52]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/64781795/duan-yongping-s-investment-has-made-five-moves-latest-comments)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/64781795/duan-yongping-s-investment-has-made-five-moves-latest-comments) [[99]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605060215)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605060215)  
- When you realize you are wrong, **correct it immediately**. [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do) [[42]](https://www.panewslab.com/en/articles/e3681069-bdd4-4d57-8370-0ea7ebbb110a)(https://www.panewslab.com/en/articles/e3681069-bdd4-4d57-8370-0ea7ebbb110a)

That philosophy came out of real mistakes. He has described losing money in an airline investment despite being directionally right on oil, because he did not understand the business deeply enough, and losing money in the UNG natural-gas ETF because he misunderstood the instrument’s structure and decay. He later said the lesson was straightforward: he had touched things he did not fully understand. [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do) The significance of those examples is not merely autobiographical. They show Duan’s philosophy is built around **error prevention** more than around prediction.

## 7) How Duan applies this in practice

### NetEase
NetEase is the clearest early example of Duan’s circle-of-competence investing. He invested when the market was highly pessimistic, believed he understood games and the company’s team, saw a favorable cash-versus-market-value setup, and acted very aggressively. He later said the case reflected deep operational understanding, not mere cheapness. [[39]](https://www.futunn.com/en/learn/detail-duan-yongping-dont-do-what-you-dont-understand-when-investing-1259-2109010075)(https://www.futunn.com/en/learn/detail-duan-yongping-dont-do-what-you-dont-understand-when-investing-1259-2109010075) [[57]](https://q.futunn.com/feed/116408150261764)(https://q.futunn.com/feed/116408150261764) [[96]](https://www.aicoin.com/en/article/499626)(https://www.aicoin.com/en/article/499626) [[100]](https://www.itiger.com/news/1182217896)(https://www.itiger.com/news/1182217896)

The mechanism here matters: a depressed price alone did not create conviction. Conviction came from the combination of operational familiarity, confidence in management, and a balance-sheet or valuation setup that offered real downside protection.

### Apple
Apple is the signature Duan holding and shows how he applies Buffett-Munger principles to technology. He has said he understood Apple’s business, culture, and user orientation well enough to hold it for many years, and that without such understanding he would have sold long ago. His thesis centers on **user value, product differentiation, ecosystem strength, and culture**, not on gadget forecasting. [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[30]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tengjiajimmyli_duan-yongping-a-chinese-american-billionaire-activity-7394915600617099264-s6EF)(https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tengjiajimmyli_duan-yongping-a-chinese-american-billionaire-activity-7394915600617099264-s6EF) [[37]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/51820977/duan-yongping-rarely-makes-an-appearance-discussing-anecdotes-about-buffett)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/51820977/duan-yongping-rarely-makes-an-appearance-discussing-anecdotes-about-buffett) [[51]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/67107835/weekend-reading-a-complete-summary-of-duan-yongping-s-2025)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/67107835/weekend-reading-a-complete-summary-of-duan-yongping-s-2025) [[96]](https://www.aicoin.com/en/article/499626)(https://www.aicoin.com/en/article/499626)

That is a crucial distinction. Duan is not claiming special skill at forecasting each hardware cycle. He is evaluating Apple as a consumer ecosystem with habit, pricing power, switching costs, and a culture of product-user alignment. In other words, he makes selected technology investable by reframing it as a **consumer franchise with durable user economics**.

### Tencent and Kweichow Moutai
Duan has repeatedly described **Apple, Tencent, and Moutai** as his conceptual core holdings. Tencent fits his framework as a business he understands relatively well; Moutai fits because of brand, habit, scarcity, and a product whose core should **not** be changed casually. [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[42]](https://www.panewslab.com/en/articles/e3681069-bdd4-4d57-8370-0ea7ebbb110a)(https://www.panewslab.com/en/articles/e3681069-bdd4-4d57-8370-0ea7ebbb110a) [[52]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/64781795/duan-yongping-s-investment-has-made-five-moves-latest-comments)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/64781795/duan-yongping-s-investment-has-made-five-moves-latest-comments) [[99]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605060215)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605060215)

These two examples also show how broad his “understanding” requirement actually is. Tencent is a modern platform business; Moutai is a branded consumer staple with cultural and scarcity elements. The common denominator is not sector but **durability that can be understood through customer behavior**.

### PDD and newer AI names
PDD is revealing because Duan has admitted it was not a pure “fully understood” case in the same way as Apple or Moutai, even though it became meaningful. That shows he sometimes allows exceptions, but he clearly distinguishes highest-conviction holdings from less certain ones. [[51]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/67107835/weekend-reading-a-complete-summary-of-duan-yongping-s-2025)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/67107835/weekend-reading-a-complete-summary-of-duan-yongping-s-2025) [[96]](https://www.aicoin.com/en/article/499626)(https://www.aicoin.com/en/article/499626)

On AI and names like NVIDIA, Google, TSM, and ASML, Duan’s recent comments show **curiosity plus caution**. He says AI is important and should not be ignored, but he also says he is still studying it and that the real question is the durability of the moat ten years out. That is a philosophy under adaptation, not abandonment. [[9]](https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891)(https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891) [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[51]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/67107835/weekend-reading-a-complete-summary-of-duan-yongping-s-2025)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/67107835/weekend-reading-a-complete-summary-of-duan-yongping-s-2025)

## 8) What is distinctive about Duan?

Duan is best understood as **Buffett-Munger translated through an entrepreneur’s operating lens**. His distinctive features are:  
- unusually strong emphasis on **culture** as an investment variable, [[9]](https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891)(https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891) [[27]](https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786)(https://www.bitget.com/news/detail/12560605057786) [[38]](https://finance.yahoo.com/news/notes-duan-yongpings-talk-stanford-160952171.html)(https://finance.yahoo.com/news/notes-duan-yongpings-talk-stanford-160952171.html)  
- a very explicit **stop-doing list**, [[9]](https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891)(https://xueqiu.com/8862627516/361235891) [[11]](https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579)(https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579) [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)  
- a competence test rooted in whether one can **sleep** through volatility, [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)  
- and greater willingness than historical Buffett/Munger to invest in selected **technology** businesses he believes he truly understands. [[30]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tengjiajimmyli_duan-yongping-a-chinese-american-billionaire-activity-7394915600617099264-s6EF)(https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tengjiajimmyli_duan-yongping-a-chinese-american-billionaire-activity-7394915600617099264-s6EF) [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do) [[51]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/67107835/weekend-reading-a-complete-summary-of-duan-yongping-s-2025)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/67107835/weekend-reading-a-complete-summary-of-duan-yongping-s-2025)

The best way to summarize his China-context adaptation is this: he keeps Buffett’s core equation of **business + value + patience**, but raises the hurdle on **trustworthiness, cultural coherence, and personal comprehension** because those variables do more of the protective work in markets where mechanical cheapness is especially unreliable.

## Warren Buffett’s investment philosophy

## 1) A stock is a business, and the goal is intrinsic value growth

Buffett’s core principle is that a stock is a **partial ownership stake in a business**, not a trading chip. Berkshire’s owner-oriented materials say shareholders should think of themselves as owners of a business, not owners of paper whose price wiggles every day. Berkshire’s economic objective is to maximize the growth of **intrinsic business value per share**, not to maximize size or market excitement. [[67]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/owners.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/owners.html) [[77]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/ownman.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/ownman.pdf)

Buffett’s definition of **intrinsic value** is the discounted value of the cash that can be taken out of a business over its remaining life. He has also stressed that intrinsic value is necessarily an estimate rather than a perfectly precise number. [[67]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/owners.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/owners.html) [[68]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1989.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1989.html) [[69]](https://s3.amazonaws.com/static.contentres.com/media/documents/8a6f3720-8d2a-4eef-b82e-420b5ca79d4c.pdf)(https://s3.amazonaws.com/static.contentres.com/media/documents/8a6f3720-8d2a-4eef-b82e-420b5ca79d4c.pdf) [[70]](https://www.cnbc.com/2018/12/05/warren-buffett-on-the-biggest-puzzle-for-investors-intrinsic-value.html)(https://www.cnbc.com/2018/12/05/warren-buffett-on-the-biggest-puzzle-for-investors-intrinsic-value.html)

That definition is more demanding than it sounds. It implies that Buffett’s real unit of analysis is not EPS, not book value in isolation, and not short-term price momentum, but the stream of cash the business can produce **over its remaining life**. That is why Buffett’s philosophy privileges businesses whose economics remain legible many years out.

## 2) From Graham-style cheapness to “wonderful businesses at fair prices”

Buffett began in a more Graham-like mode: buying statistically cheap stocks or “cigar butts.” The major evolution in Berkshire’s philosophy was toward buying **better businesses**, even if they were not the absolute cheapest on superficial metrics. Buffett later stated the mature rule clearly: **it is far better to buy a wonderful company at a fair price than a fair company at a wonderful price**. [[68]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1989.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1989.html) [[79]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/SpecialLetters/WEB%20past%20present%20future%202014.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/SpecialLetters/WEB%20past%20present%20future%202014.pdf)

Munger was central to this shift, but the resulting Buffett framework is now clear on its own terms:  
- understandable business, [[46]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html) [[141]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)  
- favorable long-term economics, [[141]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)  
- able and trustworthy management, [[141]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf) [[143]](https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1998/05/04/morning-session---1998-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)(https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1998/05/04/morning-session---1998-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)  
- sensible price. [[141]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)

That four-part structure is probably the cleanest summary of Buffett’s philosophy. [[141]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf) It also explains why Berkshire’s great winners are not random. **See’s Candies** taught Berkshire what a high-quality franchise could do; **Coca-Cola** and **American Express** demonstrated how brand and network effects could produce decades of attractive economics; **Apple** showed that Buffett’s mature framework could apply even to a technology-shaped company when the business behaved more like a consumer ecosystem than like a commodity hardware producer. [[1]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf) [[7]](https://www.lifestories.org/attachment/en/5b64a8a46aa72ce4430396a1/TextOneColumnWithFile/62b4d09ad8e78ac4930df508)(https://www.lifestories.org/attachment/en/5b64a8a46aa72ce4430396a1/TextOneColumnWithFile/62b4d09ad8e78ac4930df508)

## 3) Circle of competence: selection, not omniscience

Buffett’s canonical formulation is that investors do not need to understand every company, only those within their **circle of competence**, and that the **size** of the circle matters less than knowing its **boundaries**. [[46]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html)

He and Munger have repeatedly said that if something seems difficult to understand, that is itself useful information. In Buffett’s framing, intelligent investing is not complex, but it is highly selective. [[46]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html) [[138]](https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2002/05/04/afternoon-session---2002-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)(https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2002/05/04/afternoon-session---2002-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html) [[139]](https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1994/04/25/morning-session---1994-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)(https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1994/04/25/morning-session---1994-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)

This has practical implications: Buffett historically avoided many fast-changing technology businesses not because he thought technology was unimportant, but because he thought predicting their long-term economics was often **outside Berkshire’s perimeter**. Berkshire’s own acquisition criteria even said that if there is “lots of technology,” Berkshire likely will not understand it. [[83]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/final1999pdf.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/final1999pdf.pdf) [[122]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/1999ar/acq.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/1999ar/acq.html)

Those acquisition criteria are worth stating in full because they operationalize Buffett’s philosophy. Berkshire sought **large purchases with at least $50 million of before-tax earnings**, **consistent earning power**, **good returns on equity with little or no debt**, **management in place**, **simple businesses**, and **an offering price**. [[122]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/1999ar/acq.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/1999ar/acq.html) That list shows Buffett’s discipline was never merely abstract: Berkshire institutionalized it in concrete thresholds and filters.

## 4) Business quality: moats, pricing power, returns on capital

Buffett’s mature philosophy prefers businesses with **durable competitive advantages**, often described as moats or franchises. In Buffett’s framing, the ideal business has a product or service that is desired, hard to substitute, and not structurally trapped by poor economics, allowing the firm to earn strong returns on capital and, in many cases, raise prices over time. [[1]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf) [[141]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf) [[142]](https://www.manufacturedhomepronews.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/ValueWalkonMOATSBerkshireWarrenBuffettCharlieMungerCompetitiveAdvantagesBud-LabitanClaytonHomesPg77-81.pdf)(https://www.manufacturedhomepronews.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/ValueWalkonMOATSBerkshireWarrenBuffettCharlieMungerCompetitiveAdvantagesBud-LabitanClaytonHomesPg77-81.pdf)

His famous time test is revealing: **time is the friend of a great business and the enemy of a poor one**. If a business can earn high returns on capital for a long time, compounding works with you. If the business has weak economics, time works against you. [[143]](https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1998/05/04/morning-session---1998-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)(https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1998/05/04/morning-session---1998-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)

That is why Berkshire’s public-stock portfolio has long clustered in branded, cash-generative, high-return businesses such as **American Express, Coca-Cola, Moody’s, and Apple**. Buffett explicitly grouped those businesses as very large, highly profitable, household-name companies that earn high returns on the net tangible equity required for their operations. [[1]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)

The causal logic is central: high returns on tangible capital mean a business does **not** need to constantly consume fresh capital just to stand still. That leaves more cash available for dividends, buybacks, acquisitions, or internally funded growth. Pricing power then protects those returns against inflation and cost pressure. This is why Buffett repeatedly prefers businesses whose economics are strong **before** leverage, not businesses that look strong only because debt amplifies mediocre returns.

## 5) Management and capital allocation

Buffett looks for management that is both **able and trustworthy**. [[141]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf) [[143]](https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1998/05/04/morning-session---1998-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)(https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1998/05/04/morning-session---1998-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)

For wholly owned subsidiaries, this becomes a defining Berkshire trait: decentralized operations run by managers treated like owners, with minimal bureaucracy and long-term scorecards. Buffett has repeatedly emphasized that Berkshire’s best managers are often already wealthy and keep working because they love the business, not because they need the next bonus or title. [[2]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf) [[7]](https://www.lifestories.org/attachment/en/5b64a8a46aa72ce4430396a1/TextOneColumnWithFile/62b4d09ad8e78ac4930df508)(https://www.lifestories.org/attachment/en/5b64a8a46aa72ce4430396a1/TextOneColumnWithFile/62b4d09ad8e78ac4930df508) [[83]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/final1999pdf.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/final1999pdf.pdf) [[141]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)

This is not separate from investing; it is part of the investment thesis. A high-quality business with poor capital allocation can still destroy value, while a good manager can enhance value by reinvesting well, buying back shares sensibly, and avoiding empire-building. [[1]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf) [[2]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf) [[141]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)

Buffett’s edge here is broader than security selection. He treats Berkshire itself as a capital-allocation machine that can shift among **public equities, whole-company acquisitions, buybacks, insurance float, and retained earnings**. That flexibility is one reason his philosophy scales institutionally better than a pure stock-picking doctrine.

## 6) Risk: permanent loss, not volatility

Buffett’s philosophy sharply distinguishes **risk** from normal stock-price volatility. Berkshire’s long-standing rule is to avoid **permanent loss of capital**, and Buffett and Munger have criticized academic finance for treating volatility itself as risk. [[44]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2023ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2023ltr.pdf) [[149]](https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1997/05/05/morning-session---1997-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)(https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1997/05/05/morning-session---1997-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)

This leads directly to a conservative balance-sheet philosophy:  
- avoid excessive leverage, [[46]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html) [[77]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/ownman.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/ownman.pdf)  
- maintain abundant liquidity, [[2]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf) [[4]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/qtrly/1stqtr25.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/qtrly/1stqtr25.pdf) [[77]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/ownman.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/ownman.pdf)  
- do not rely on the kindness of strangers for refinancing, [[46]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html) [[75]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)  
- and preserve the ability to act during crises. [[2]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf) [[20]](https://steadycompounding.com/transcript/brk-2025/)(https://steadycompounding.com/transcript/brk-2025/) [[77]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/ownman.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/ownman.pdf)

The point is structural freedom. Berkshire wants to be in a position where market panic creates options rather than obligations. [[2]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf) [[20]](https://steadycompounding.com/transcript/brk-2025/)(https://steadycompounding.com/transcript/brk-2025/) [[75]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf) [[77]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/ownman.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/ownman.pdf)

A useful real-world contrast is the 2021 collapse of **Archegos**, where leverage turned adverse price movement into forced liquidation and produced more than **$5 billion** of losses at Credit Suisse alone. Buffett’s anti-leverage stance is designed precisely to avoid that kind of ruin dynamic: volatility is survivable; margin calls are not.

## 7) Patience, inactivity, and concentration

Buffett’s style is famously episodic. He has said Berkshire often finds that **nothing looks compelling**, and only rarely becomes “knee-deep in opportunities.” [[1]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)

That makes patience central rather than optional. At the 2025 annual meeting, Buffett said Berkshire has made a great deal of money by **not wanting to be fully invested at all times**. In a 2026 interview, he said that if Berkshire does not see enough attractive investments, **it simply does nothing**. [[5]](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/02/cnbc-transcript-berkshire-hathaway-chairman-warren-buffett-sits-down-with-cnbcs-becky-quick-during-the-2026-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting-today-.html)(https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/02/cnbc-transcript-berkshire-hathaway-chairman-warren-buffett-sits-down-with-cnbcs-becky-quick-during-the-2026-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting-today-.html) [[20]](https://steadycompounding.com/transcript/brk-2025/)(https://steadycompounding.com/transcript/brk-2025/)

This interacts with concentration. Buffett has long defended concentration when conviction is high, saying diversification mainly protects against ignorance. Berkshire’s equity portfolio, despite the conglomerate’s broader operating diversification, has often been highly concentrated in a few top positions. [[84]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1984.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1984.html) [[105]](https://valuesider.com/guru/warren-buffett-berkshire-hathaway/portfolio)(https://valuesider.com/guru/warren-buffett-berkshire-hathaway/portfolio) [[147]](https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1996/05/06/afternoon-session---1996-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)(https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1996/05/06/afternoon-session---1996-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)

But Buffett is equally clear that scale changes the game. Berkshire itself acknowledged in 1999 that “truly large superiorities” over the index were harder because its capital base had grown so large that many smaller opportunities could no longer matter. [[121]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/1999ar/1999ar.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/1999ar/1999ar.pdf) This is not a minor footnote; it is one of the most important limitations of Buffett-style investing at Berkshire’s size. A philosophy can remain intact even while the set of opportunities large enough to move results shrinks materially.

## 8) How Buffett’s philosophy looks in 2024–2026

### Huge cash and Treasury-bill balances
Berkshire’s recent cash/T-bill position has triggered debate, but Berkshire’s own explanation is philosophically consistent: large liquidity reflects a mix of **insurance support, resilience, and dry powder**, not a shift away from business ownership. Greg Abel explicitly wrote that Berkshire will always prefer owning productive businesses to U.S. Treasuries, but will remain patient and disciplined until the right opportunities appear. [[2]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf) [[4]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/qtrly/1stqtr25.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/qtrly/1stqtr25.pdf)

As of 2024–2025, the balance was not just “large” but **well above $300 billion**, which is why the debate became intense. The key analytical point is that higher short-term rates make waiting less costly, but they do **not** eliminate the opportunity cost of failing to find productive long-duration assets.

### Apple reductions
Berkshire’s significant reduction in Apple did not mean Buffett stopped admiring the company. Recent meeting commentary indicated tax considerations and position size mattered, even as Buffett continued to praise Tim Cook and Apple’s business quality. This shows Buffett’s philosophy is not “buy forever no matter what”; it is **business quality plus valuation, taxes, and opportunity cost**. [[20]](https://steadycompounding.com/transcript/brk-2025/)(https://steadycompounding.com/transcript/brk-2025/) [[105]](https://valuesider.com/guru/warren-buffett-berkshire-hathaway/portfolio)(https://valuesider.com/guru/warren-buffett-berkshire-hathaway/portfolio) [[107]](https://www.nbcconnecticut.com/news/business/money-report/buffetts-berkshire-hikes-stakes-in-five-japanese-trading-houses-to-almost-10-each/3521921/)(https://www.nbcconnecticut.com/news/business/money-report/buffetts-berkshire-hikes-stakes-in-five-japanese-trading-houses-to-almost-10-each/3521921/) [[108]](https://hedgefundalpha.com/conferences/2025-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting/)(https://hedgefundalpha.com/conferences/2025-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting/)

### Japanese trading houses
The Japanese trading houses illustrate classic Buffett thinking in a modern setting: understandable, diversified businesses bought at attractive prices, financed partly with low-cost yen debt, and intended to be held for decades. Buffett explicitly said he expected Greg Abel and his successors to hold them for many decades. [[1]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf) [[20]](https://steadycompounding.com/transcript/brk-2025/)(https://steadycompounding.com/transcript/brk-2025/) [[104]](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1067983/000119312525054885/d810841dars.pdf)(https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1067983/000119312525054885/d810841dars.pdf) [[107]](https://www.nbcconnecticut.com/news/business/money-report/buffetts-berkshire-hikes-stakes-in-five-japanese-trading-houses-to-almost-10-each/3521921/)(https://www.nbcconnecticut.com/news/business/money-report/buffetts-berkshire-hikes-stakes-in-five-japanese-trading-houses-to-almost-10-each/3521921/)

The mechanism is textbook Buffett: buy a diversified set of cash-generative businesses at sensible valuations, fund part of the position with appropriately matched low-cost liabilities, and then let time and retained earnings do most of the work.

### Succession to Greg Abel
The most important recent development is that Berkshire has formalized the continuity of Buffett-Munger philosophy through Greg Abel. Berkshire’s proxy states Abel became CEO on January 1, 2026, while Buffett remained chairman, and Abel’s first letter explicitly says Berkshire’s culture and values remain unchanged. [[2]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf) [[3]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/meet01/2026proxystatement.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/meet01/2026proxystatement.pdf)

That does not mean the future will look identical. It means the **decision architecture**—owner mindset, balance-sheet conservatism, decentralization, and selective deployment—has been institutionalized beyond Buffett personally.

## 9) What is distinctive about Buffett?

Buffett is the most complete **capital allocator** of the three. His philosophy combines:  
- business valuation, [[67]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/owners.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/owners.html) [[68]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1989.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1989.html)  
- moats and management judgment, [[141]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2007ltr.pdf) [[143]](https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1998/05/04/morning-session---1998-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)(https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1998/05/04/morning-session---1998-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)  
- balance-sheet conservatism, [[2]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf) [[77]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/ownman.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/ownman.pdf)  
- tax awareness, [[108]](https://hedgefundalpha.com/conferences/2025-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting/)(https://hedgefundalpha.com/conferences/2025-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting/)  
- and the ability to move between public stocks, private acquisitions, insurance float, buybacks, and retained earnings. [[1]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf) [[2]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2025ltr.pdf) [[77]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/ownman.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/ownman.pdf)

That institutional dimension makes Buffett’s philosophy broader than a personal stock-picking method. He is not just choosing securities; he is designing a structure that maximizes the number of ways capital can be deployed intelligently while minimizing the chance of forced error.

## Charlie Munger’s investment philosophy

## 1) Investing as rational decision-making, not narrow finance

Munger’s philosophy is broader than stock selection. He argues that good investing requires a **latticework of mental models from multiple disciplines**—not just accounting or valuation, but also psychology, incentives, engineering, probability, biology, and human behavior. [[6]](https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf)(https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf) [[71]](https://speakola.com/corp/charli-munger-widom-business-usc-1994)(https://speakola.com/corp/charli-munger-widom-business-usc-1994) [[72]](https://www.gould.usc.edu/news/300-graduate-from-usc-law/)(https://www.gould.usc.edu/news/300-graduate-from-usc-law/)

His complaint is that investors who memorize isolated facts without a theory structure do not really possess usable knowledge. [[71]](https://speakola.com/corp/charli-munger-widom-business-usc-1994)(https://speakola.com/corp/charli-munger-widom-business-usc-1994)

That makes Munger the most **epistemic** of the three: he is less focused on one formula than on how to build a mind that avoids standard human errors. [[6]](https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf)(https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf) [[71]](https://speakola.com/corp/charli-munger-widom-business-usc-1994)(https://speakola.com/corp/charli-munger-widom-business-usc-1994) [[73]](https://jamesclear.com/great-speeches/psychology-of-human-misjudgment-by-charlie-munger)(https://jamesclear.com/great-speeches/psychology-of-human-misjudgment-by-charlie-munger) [[74]](https://fs.blog/great-talks/psychology-human-misjudgment/)(https://fs.blog/great-talks/psychology-human-misjudgment/)

The analytical consequence is important: Munger is not anti-valuation; he is anti-**single-model thinking**. A business can look cheap in accounting terms and still be unattractive once incentives, industry structure, consumer behavior, and error rates are taken seriously.

## 2) Inversion: avoid stupidity first

Munger’s most famous practical tool is **inversion**—thinking first about how to avoid obvious errors. He often framed this with the line about wanting to know where he is going to die so he can never go there, and Buffett later explicitly linked that style of thinking to Berkshire’s decision-making. [[45]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1985.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1985.html) [[75]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)

In investment terms, inversion means asking:  
- What would permanently impair capital? [[46]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html) [[75]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)  
- What kinds of businesses are structurally too hard to predict? [[75]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)  
- Where would leverage or bad incentives make a bad outcome unrecoverable? [[73]](https://jamesclear.com/great-speeches/psychology-of-human-misjudgment-by-charlie-munger)(https://jamesclear.com/great-speeches/psychology-of-human-misjudgment-by-charlie-munger) [[150]](https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2008/05/03/afternoon-session---2008-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)(https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2008/05/03/afternoon-session---2008-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)  
- Which mistakes are common, tempting, and avoidable? [[6]](https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf)(https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf) [[73]](https://jamesclear.com/great-speeches/psychology-of-human-misjudgment-by-charlie-munger)(https://jamesclear.com/great-speeches/psychology-of-human-misjudgment-by-charlie-munger) [[74]](https://fs.blog/great-talks/psychology-human-misjudgment/)(https://fs.blog/great-talks/psychology-human-misjudgment/)

This is why Munger’s philosophy can look conservative even when his portfolios are concentrated: he wants a **few situations where the odds are strongly favorable**, not many situations where mediocre analysis is spread around. [[6]](https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf)(https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf) [[154]](https://www.wealest.com/articles/circle-of-competence)(https://www.wealest.com/articles/circle-of-competence)

## 3) Quality-first investing, and Munger’s influence on Buffett

Munger’s most important practical influence on Berkshire was pushing Buffett away from pure cigar-butt investing toward **good businesses at fair prices**. In his own retrospective telling, Buffett had once made a lot of money buying very cheap, often mediocre companies, but Munger believed that approach was self-limiting and that far more money could be made by owning superior businesses with durable economics. [[7]](https://www.lifestories.org/attachment/en/5b64a8a46aa72ce4430396a1/TextOneColumnWithFile/62b4d09ad8e78ac4930df508)(https://www.lifestories.org/attachment/en/5b64a8a46aa72ce4430396a1/TextOneColumnWithFile/62b4d09ad8e78ac4930df508) [[78]](https://rationalwalk.com/an-introduction-to-charlie-mungers-investment-philosophy/)(https://rationalwalk.com/an-introduction-to-charlie-mungers-investment-philosophy/) [[79]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/SpecialLetters/WEB%20past%20present%20future%202014.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/SpecialLetters/WEB%20past%20present%20future%202014.pdf)

Munger repeatedly cited **See’s Candies** as the pivotal case: it showed Berkshire that a great brand, a strong culture, and customer attachment could create economics far better than what a purely asset-based or statistically cheap framework would reveal. He even said See’s taught Buffett why Coca-Cola was such a powerful business. [[7]](https://www.lifestories.org/attachment/en/5b64a8a46aa72ce4430396a1/TextOneColumnWithFile/62b4d09ad8e78ac4930df508)(https://www.lifestories.org/attachment/en/5b64a8a46aa72ce4430396a1/TextOneColumnWithFile/62b4d09ad8e78ac4930df508)

This is the clearest place where Munger differs in emphasis from Buffett. Buffett certainly agrees with quality-first investing, but Munger is more likely to explain **why a superior business deserves a higher multiple**: not because valuation no longer matters, but because the combination of long runway, low capital intensity, and durable customer behavior can overwhelm the arithmetic advantage of a merely cheap mediocre company.

## 4) Human misjudgment, incentives, and psychology

Munger’s psychology work is not a side hobby; it is central to his investment philosophy. He believed many errors come from recurring human tendencies such as **incentive-caused bias, denial, social proof, commitment bias, authority effects, and misapplied models**. [[73]](https://jamesclear.com/great-speeches/psychology-of-human-misjudgment-by-charlie-munger)(https://jamesclear.com/great-speeches/psychology-of-human-misjudgment-by-charlie-munger) [[74]](https://fs.blog/great-talks/psychology-human-misjudgment/)(https://fs.blog/great-talks/psychology-human-misjudgment/)

His first rule in this area is to **never underestimate incentives**. If incentives are wrong, even intelligent people can reason badly. [[73]](https://jamesclear.com/great-speeches/psychology-of-human-misjudgment-by-charlie-munger)(https://jamesclear.com/great-speeches/psychology-of-human-misjudgment-by-charlie-munger) [[74]](https://fs.blog/great-talks/psychology-human-misjudgment/)(https://fs.blog/great-talks/psychology-human-misjudgment/)

This has direct investing implications: management quality must be judged through incentive structures, analysts must guard against self-deception, and markets can become collectively irrational for long periods. [[73]](https://jamesclear.com/great-speeches/psychology-of-human-misjudgment-by-charlie-munger)(https://jamesclear.com/great-speeches/psychology-of-human-misjudgment-by-charlie-munger) [[74]](https://fs.blog/great-talks/psychology-human-misjudgment/)(https://fs.blog/great-talks/psychology-human-misjudgment/)

A useful real-world illustration is **Wells Fargo’s fake-accounts scandal**. Incentives tied too tightly to cross-selling helped drive widespread misconduct, eventually contributing to a **$3.7 billion** CFPB settlement in 2022 and destroying the “trustworthy franchise” image that once made the bank attractive to long-term owners. That is a very Munger-esque lesson: the numbers alone were not enough; the incentive system eventually corrupted the economics.

## 5) Concentration, opportunity cost, and “a few great bets”

Munger is the most explicitly pro-concentration of the three. He repeatedly argued that you do not need many ideas to get rich, only a few situations where you know something is better than average and have the courage to act. [[6]](https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf)(https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf) [[114]](https://www.benzinga.com/news/management/25/05/45248740/charlie-munger-says-the-secret-is-to-find-costcos-not-good-exits-saying-his-wealth-came-from-relentless-holding-and-not-slick-sell-signals)(https://www.benzinga.com/news/management/25/05/45248740/charlie-munger-says-the-secret-is-to-find-costcos-not-good-exits-saying-his-wealth-came-from-relentless-holding-and-not-slick-sell-signals) [[115]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/benzinga_charlie-munger-never-believed-you-needed-activity-7419227197833601024-wREe)(https://www.linkedin.com/posts/benzinga_charlie-munger-never-believed-you-needed-activity-7419227197833601024-wREe) [[154]](https://www.wealest.com/articles/circle-of-competence)(https://www.wealest.com/articles/circle-of-competence)

This is tied to **opportunity cost**. If you already have one clearly superior use of capital, many other possible investments can be rejected quickly. That is why Munger preferred waiting for rare, high-conviction situations and then moving heavily. [[6]](https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf)(https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf) [[14]](https://thetranscript.substack.com/p/12-04-2023-farewell-to-munger)(https://thetranscript.substack.com/p/12-04-2023-farewell-to-munger) [[143]](https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1998/05/04/morning-session---1998-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)(https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/1998/05/04/morning-session---1998-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html) [[154]](https://www.wealest.com/articles/circle-of-competence)(https://www.wealest.com/articles/circle-of-competence)

The practical implication is not simply “be concentrated.” It is that concentration is justified **only after** the investor has done enough work to rule out common failure modes. Munger’s version of concentration is concentrated **after inversion**, not concentrated from excitement.

## 6) Temperament: patience plus opportunism

Munger’s temperament ideal is not merely patience; it is **patience combined with opportunism**. He has said simple investing principles work especially well when the investor has the temperament to wait for the right opportunity and then act decisively. [[1]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf) [[6]](https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf)(https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf) [[158]](https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2016/04/30/afternoon-session---2016-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)(https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2016/04/30/afternoon-session---2016-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html) [[159]](https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2006/05/06/afternoon-session---2006-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)(https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2006/05/06/afternoon-session---2006-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)

He also thought inactivity is emotionally harder than it sounds. Long stretches of doing little are part of the process, not evidence that the process is broken. [[159]](https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2006/05/06/afternoon-session---2006-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)(https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2006/05/06/afternoon-session---2006-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)

This makes Munger less of a “stock picker” in the conventional sense and more of a **high-threshold decision-maker**. He would rather be inactive for months or years than dilute attention across mediocre situations.

## 7) How Munger’s philosophy looks in practice

### Costco
Munger praised Costco as nearly a “perfect” company because of its culture, low-markup model, customer trust, and sensible compensation practices. He openly acknowledged the stock’s high valuation, but his admiration shows how strongly he prioritized business quality and culture. [[98]](https://steadycompounding.com/transcript/djco23/)(https://steadycompounding.com/transcript/djco23/) [[117]](https://www.kingswell.io/p/charlie-munger-q-and-a-2023-daily)(https://www.kingswell.io/p/charlie-munger-q-and-a-2023-daily)

### BYD
BYD became one of the best-known examples of Munger’s willingness to back an unconventional but high-conviction idea heavily, including outside the U.S. [[97]](https://markets.businessinsider.com/news/stocks/charlie-munger-daily-journal-final-stock-portfolio-investing-byd-alibaba-2024-1)(https://markets.businessinsider.com/news/stocks/charlie-munger-daily-journal-final-stock-portfolio-investing-byd-alibaba-2024-1) [[114]](https://www.benzinga.com/news/management/25/05/45248740/charlie-munger-says-the-secret-is-to-find-costcos-not-good-exits-saying-his-wealth-came-from-relentless-holding-and-not-slick-sell-signals)(https://www.benzinga.com/news/management/25/05/45248740/charlie-munger-says-the-secret-is-to-find-costcos-not-good-exits-saying-his-wealth-came-from-relentless-holding-and-not-slick-sell-signals)

### Daily Journal portfolio
The Daily Journal portfolio showed classic Munger traits: extreme concentration, very low turnover, and willingness to hold for years. [[97]](https://markets.businessinsider.com/news/stocks/charlie-munger-daily-journal-final-stock-portfolio-investing-byd-alibaba-2024-1)(https://markets.businessinsider.com/news/stocks/charlie-munger-daily-journal-final-stock-portfolio-investing-byd-alibaba-2024-1) [[118]](https://13f.info/13f/000143774925004092-daily-journal-corp-q4-2024)(https://13f.info/13f/000143774925004092-daily-journal-corp-q4-2024) [[119]](https://ir.dailyjournal.com/hubfs/djco/governance/DJCO_ANNL2024.pdf)(https://ir.dailyjournal.com/hubfs/djco/governance/DJCO_ANNL2024.pdf)

### Alibaba mistake
Munger’s Alibaba experience is equally revealing. He later called it one of his worst mistakes, explaining that he overestimated the economics of what was still, in important respects, a highly competitive retail business. This is classic Munger too: he publicly analyzed the mistake in terms of moat misjudgment, not in terms of bad luck. [[98]](https://steadycompounding.com/transcript/djco23/)(https://steadycompounding.com/transcript/djco23/) [[120]](https://www.piranhaprofits.com/blog/charlie-munger-alibaba-mistake)(https://www.piranhaprofits.com/blog/charlie-munger-alibaba-mistake)

That admission is analytically valuable because it shows the limitation of even a strong framework: if the investor misclassifies the underlying business economics—treating a highly competitive retail engine as though it had stronger structural protection than it really did—concentration magnifies the cost of the error.

## 8) What is distinctive about Munger?

Munger’s philosophy is the deepest on **how to think**, not just what to buy. His distinctive contributions are:  
- multidisciplinary mental models, [[6]](https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf)(https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf) [[71]](https://speakola.com/corp/charli-munger-widom-business-usc-1994)(https://speakola.com/corp/charli-munger-widom-business-usc-1994) [[72]](https://www.gould.usc.edu/news/300-graduate-from-usc-law/)(https://www.gould.usc.edu/news/300-graduate-from-usc-law/)  
- inversion and error avoidance, [[45]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1985.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1985.html) [[74]](https://fs.blog/great-talks/psychology-human-misjudgment/)(https://fs.blog/great-talks/psychology-human-misjudgment/) [[75]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2009ltr.pdf)  
- incentives and psychology, [[73]](https://jamesclear.com/great-speeches/psychology-of-human-misjudgment-by-charlie-munger)(https://jamesclear.com/great-speeches/psychology-of-human-misjudgment-by-charlie-munger) [[74]](https://fs.blog/great-talks/psychology-human-misjudgment/)(https://fs.blog/great-talks/psychology-human-misjudgment/)

## Comparative synthesis: common foundation, real differences

The overlap among the three is strong enough that an investor can think of them as sharing one philosophical base and differing mainly in **implementation emphasis**.

| Dimension | Duan Yongping | Warren Buffett | Charlie Munger | Practical takeaway |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Core object of investing | Business understood through product, users, culture | Business valued through intrinsic value and capital allocation | Decision under uncertainty using multiple models | Start with the business, not the ticker |
| Circle of competence | Very narrow, explicitly enforced by a stop-doing list | Important, but embedded in institutional opportunity set | Important, with special stress on knowing the boundary | Boundary recognition beats breadth |
| Business quality | Business model + culture + user value | Moat + returns on capital + pricing power | Superior economics + quality of incentives | Quality precedes valuation work |
| Valuation | DCF as a way of thinking; cognitive + price margin of safety | Intrinsic value estimate with price discipline | Opportunity-cost driven; pay up for exceptional quality if justified | Cheapness alone is inadequate |
| Management assessment | Culture, founder/operator quality, user alignment | Ability, integrity, capital allocation | Incentives, rationality, misjudgment risk | Judge people through behavior and incentives |
| Technology | More open if personally understood | Historically cautious, but adaptive in Apple-like cases | Historically cautious; quality-first, not sector-first | Tech is investable only when economics are legible |
| Concentration | Very concentrated in high-conviction names | Concentrated in equities, diversified at conglomerate level | Most explicit defender of concentration | Concentrate only after deep filtering |
| Risk definition | Not understanding what you own; inability to sleep | Permanent capital loss, not volatility | Ruin, stupidity, bad incentives, leverage | Avoid forced error, not mere fluctuation |
| Time horizon | Multi-year to decade-plus | Indefinite when economics persist | Very long, with rare decisive moves | Patience is an input, not a virtue signal |
| Macro stance | Mostly bottom-up | Mostly bottom-up | Mostly bottom-up, anti-forecasting | Forecast less; filter better |
| Best use for investors | Operator-led, culture-heavy evaluation | Core blueprint for most investors | Judgment training and error reduction | Learn Buffett first, Munger second, Duan as an applied overlay |

### 1) What they share

Across all three, the common foundation is unusually strong.

#### Owner mindset
All three insist that investing means **owning businesses**, not trading symbols. [[1]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/2024ltr.pdf) [[7]](https://www.lifestories.org/attachment/en/5b64a8a46aa72ce4430396a1/TextOneColumnWithFile/62b4d09ad8e78ac4930df508)(https://www.lifestories.org/attachment/en/5b64a8a46aa72ce4430396a1/TextOneColumnWithFile/62b4d09ad8e78ac4930df508) [[8]](https://xueqiu.com/u/1247347556)(https://xueqiu.com/u/1247347556) [[11]](https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579)(https://xueqiu.com/8959246745/317456579) [[67]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/owners.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/owners.html)

#### Circle of competence
All three believe the investor should operate only where understanding is real, and that boundary recognition matters more than breadth. [[36]](https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do)(https://news.futunn.com/en/post/58710159/weekend-reading-duan-yongping-s-three-major-investment-principles-do) [[38]](https://finance.yahoo.com/news/notes-duan-yongpings-talk-stanford-160952171.html)(https://finance.yahoo.com/news/notes-duan-yongpings-talk-stanford-160952171.html) [[46]](https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html)(https://www.berkshirehathaway.com/letters/1996.html) [[138]](https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2002/05/04/afternoon-session---2002-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)(https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2002/05/04/afternoon-session---2002-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html)

#### Quality + value, not cheapness alone
All three reject the idea that a low price by itself is enough. The business must be understandable, durable, and sensibly priced. #### Management matters
They all care deeply about management quality, but in different language: Buffett emphasizes ability and trustworthiness, Munger emphasizes incentives and rationality, and Duan emphasizes culture and user orientation.
